name: blake2b_24_sealed_nonce
description: libsodium sealed-box nonce derivation: BLAKE2b with 24-byte output over (ephemeral_pubkey || recipient_pubkey). Pins the libsodium-compat formula — Dina's pre-#9 Go implementation used truncated SHA-512(24) which broke interop with every other libsodium binding; this vector proves the repair.

producer.reference_implementation_note: Matches libsodium's crypto_box_seal nonce derivation — see core/internal/adapter/crypto/nacl.go:sealNonce (Go) and tweetnacl-js / libsodium-wrappers-based NaCl.seal on the TS side.

notes: libsodium's `crypto_box_seal` derives the nonce deterministically so the recipient can recompute it from the ephemeral pub (carried in the ciphertext envelope) + its own pub. Without this, the recipient can't know the nonce the sender used.

Input layout: ephemeral_pubkey (32 bytes) concatenated with recipient_pubkey (32 bytes). Both are X25519 public keys (raw, not compressed). Concatenation order is (ephemeral, recipient) — swapping them produces a different hash and breaks interop.
BLAKE2b parameters: plain 'BLAKE2b' (not BLAKE2bp, not keyed, not salted, no personalisation). Only the output length is customised — 24 bytes.

Historical note: pre-#9 Go Dina computed the nonce as SHA-512(input)[:24]. That produces different bytes and breaks interop with every libsodium binding (Python PyNaCl, mobile native sodium, JS tweetnacl). The BLAKE2b(24) formula is what libsodium actually implements — this vector is the canonical test that catches a regression back to the SHA-512 form.
